FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD baseline data (Release 3.0), N=10,691 participants aged 9-10 years from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen Media Activity (SMA): total hours on six specific activities - TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting on device, social networking sites, video-chatting

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effect models with SMA as predictor of externalizing behaviors, psychopathology, sleep, and cognition; JIVE component as predictor with multiple outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: β = 0.107, P = 0.002 for SMA association; β = 0.117, P = 0.002 for externalizing psychopathology

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis with JIVE dimension reduction and linear mixed-effect regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions 2022

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design with SMA as independent variable (not dependent variable). Also includes non-demographic predictors (brain structural covariation patterns). E7 does not apply.
notes: Latent structural covariation pattern from JIVE extracted and used as predictor with separately reportable coefficient

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD cohort baseline data, N=11,834 children ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Recreational screen time measured in hours: weighted average of weekday and weekend screen-based activities (watching television, video games)

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects model with screen activity as fixed effect predicting general p factor; multiple linear regression models

FT_IC4.evidence: Screen Activity coefficient β = 0.116, P < 2.00E-16 in mixed-effects model (Table VIII); significant regression coefficients reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: confirmatory factor analysis extracting latent p factor, then regression analyses with behavioral and demographic predictors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in 2021 IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable predicting p factor (not dependent variable). Includes other independent variables beyond demographics (neurocognition, mental health outcomes). E7 does not apply.
notes: Mixed-effects model includes family/site random effects and gender, screen activity, sleep, physical activity, household income as fixed effects

FT_IC1.evidence: Commentary describing the ABCD study; states ABCD enrolled nearly 12,000 youth aged 9-10 across U.S.

FT_IC2.evidence: Commentary mentions screen time monitoring as a novel technology/data type in Table 1 but does not measure or analyze any specific smartphone or social media variable

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical tests reported linking SM/phone variable to non-SM outcomes. This is a resource overview, not an analysis paper.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric values (β, OR, p-values) reported for smartphone/SM associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Commentary/editorial describing ABCD study infrastructure, data resources, and responsible data use principles. No original empirical analyses conducted.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Journal of Adolescent Health 2021

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT-IC3 and FT-IC5 (not an empirical analysis); FT-EC7 not applicable to editorials/commentaries
notes: This is a special-issue commentary without original analysis. Fails inclusion at FT-IC5 level.
